coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their photocatalytic hydrogen evolution application
coredata.dc:creator: Gurel, Ari
Avignon, Frédéric
Wang, Guillaume
Lau, Stéphanie
Piquemal, Jean-Yves
Perruchot, Christian
Schaming, Delphine
coredata.dc:description: A very simple electrochemical top-down procedure was employed to obtain pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in water and using only graphite as carbonaceous precursor. The graphitic structure of the GQDs has been clearly observed by high-resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM). Then, the synthesis of N-doped GQDs was allowed by the addition of ammonia in the solution. The nitrogen doping was plainly evidenced by X-ray photoelectron (XPS) and Raman spectroscopies. The role of the electrolytic solution employed during the synthesis has been also discussed. Finally, these N-doped and non-doped GQDs were further used to prepare hybrids by grafting them onto ZnO semi-conductors, and their photocatalytic properties towards water-splitting were investigated. Interestingly, a very important enhancement of the amount of dihydrogen produced was observed with N-doped GQDs, compared to ZnO alone or to hybrids prepared with non-doped GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Water-splitting
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of GQDs and N-GQDs
Syntheses in water vs. in ethanol

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Agence de l'Environnement et de la Maîtrise de l'Energie

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Universit\u00E9 Paris Cit\u00E9 and CNRS (Centre National de Recherche Scientifique). This project has received funding from the city of Paris (Emergence Project). Ari Gurel thanks DGA (Direction G\u00E9n\u00E9rale de l'Armement) and ADEME (Agence de l'Environnement et de la Ma\u00EEtrise de l'Energie) for his Ph.D fellowship. The authors would like to thank Philippe Decorse for XPS measurements. XPS equipment was funded by the R\u00E9gion Ile-de-France, convention SESAME n\u00B016016303 and the Labex SEAM (Science and Engineering for Advanced Materials and devices).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of non-doped GQDs (A, B and C) and N-GQDs (D, E and F). The red traces on HRTEM correspond to lattice spacings of ca. 2.13 and 2.04 Å assigned to {100} and {011} planes of graphite (P63/mmc space group).
Raman spectra of pristine graphite (black), non-doped GQDs (blue) and N-GQDs (red). Measurements were performed under excitation at 633 nm.
XPS spectra of non-doped GQDs (A) and N-GQDs (B). The signal from carbon has been normalized to allow a better comparison between the two samples.
(A) High resolution XPS N(1s) spectrum of N-GQDs. (B) Schematic illustration showing the different possible environments of nitrogen atoms in the N-GQDs.
High resolution XPS C(1s) spectra of (A) non-doped GQDs and (B) N-GQDs.

TEM images of (A) GQDs obtained by electrochemistry using graphite electrodes in EtOH + NaOH and (B) particles obtained by electrochemistry using platinum electrodes in EtOH + NaOH. Fluorescence spectra measured at different wavelengths of (C) particles obtained by electrochemistry using platinum electrodes in EtOH + NaOH and (D) GQDs obtained by electrochemistry using graphite electrodes in water.
Tauc plots for ZnO, ZnO/GQDs and ZnO/N-GQDs materials.
Energy band diagram and proposed mechanism of the charge carrier transfers in ZnO/GQDs hybrids towards photocatalytic protons reduction.
Relative proportions of carbon bonds measured by XPS in GQDs and N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their photocatalytic hydrogen evolution application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ITODYS, UMR 7086 CNRS, Université Paris Cité, 15 rue Jean-Antoine de Baïf, Paris 75013, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aITODYS, UMR 7086 CNRS, Université Paris Cité, 15 rue Jean-Antoine de Baïf, Paris 75013, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MPQ, UMR 7162 CNRS, Université Paris Cité, 10 rue Alice Domon et Léonie Duquet, Paris 75013, France

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 10 rue Alice Domon et Léonie Duquet

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMPQ, UMR 7162 CNRS, Université Paris Cité, 10 rue Alice Domon et Léonie Duquet, Paris 75013, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A very simple electrochemical top-down procedure was employed to obtain pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in water and using only graphite as carbonaceous precursor. The graphitic structure of the GQDs has been clearly observed by high-resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM). Then, the synthesis of N-doped GQDs was allowed by the addition of ammonia in the solution. The nitrogen doping was plainly evidenced by X-ray photoelectron (XPS) and Raman spectroscopies. The role of the electrolytic solution employed during the synthesis has been also discussed. Finally, these N-doped and non-doped GQDs were further used to prepare hybrids by grafting them onto ZnO semi-conductors, and their photocatalytic properties towards water-splitting were investigated. Interestingly, a very important enhancement of the amount of dihydrogen produced was observed with N-doped GQDs, compared to ZnO alone or to hybrids prepared with non-doped GQDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist in a new family of nanosized carbonaceous particles displaying very enticing optical properties, such as an intense absorption and a powerful emission [1]. Their serendipitous discovery, occurred while purifying carbon nanotubes, was reported for the first time in 2004[2] but GQDs were purposely synthesized by Sun et al. two years later [3]. It is in this report that their characteristics were for the first time unveiled and their peculiar fluorescence properties described. In the following years, several articles relative to these nanoparticles and their properties were published. Indeed, due to their appealing properties, GQDs have managed to find a place in many different applications. The biggest appeal comes from the possibility of integrating their optical properties, comparable to those of other quantum dots, with all the advantages of carbon-based materials. Their optical properties have been in particular exploited for chemical sensing [4,5], bioimaging [6–8], optoelectronic devices [9,10] photodynamic therapies [11,12]

Thanks to their capabilities as electron donors and acceptors, they have also generated a huge interest towards their possible usage for catalytic applications. Apart from being low-cost and generally non-toxic, the adaptability of their surface and the possibility to interact with other species make them very suitable for this purpose. In addition, once again their optical properties play a fundamental role in this context, as these allow GQDs to convert solar energy and redirect it towards other processes. In particular, they have been associated with many materials such as Fe2O3 [13], CoO [14], CdS [15], MoS2 [16], TiO2 [17,18], ZnO [19,20], WO3 [21,22], polyoxometalates [23], gold nanoparticles [24] or C3N4 [25] and the interaction efficiency of the composite formed by GQDs and catalyst was observed for instance through the degradation of organic pollutants or in water splitting processes. GQDs have been generally simply used as photosensitizers, so to enhance the collection of visible photons of these other renown catalysts. Indeed, the catalytic process was enhanced by the presence of GQDs, thanks to their efficient light absorption and the good alignment of the energy bands of GQDs and catalyst allowing for a photoexcited electron to be transferred from carbon nanoparticles to the active material [26,27].

In recent years, much effort has been devoted to doping GQDs with heteroatoms. Indeed, heteroatom-doped GQDs are generally presented in the literature as having better optical and catalytical properties [28,29]. In 2012, Ma et al. have in particular evidenced for the first time the enhancement of photocatalytic properties of nitrogen-doped GQDs compared to non-doped GQDs, for the photo-degradation of methyl orange under visible light [30]. Since then, several studies have highlighted the particularly promising catalytic activity of N-doped GQDs [31–37]. However, such N-doped GQDs are generally synthesized from bottom-up strategies consisting of pyrolysis by hydrothermal or microwave methods of small-sized organic molecules such as glucose or citric acid in the presence of nitrogenous molecules such as urea or ethylenediamine to ensure doping. Nevertheless, several recent studies have highlighted the formation of fluorescent organic molecules during such bottom-up N-GQDs synthesis [38,39] making it difficult to properly attribute the catalytic properties to GQDs.

In this context, we will present in this work the synthesis of non-doped GQDs and N-doped GQDs from an electrochemical top-down strategy conducted in water and using graphite and eventually ammonia as precursors, without any organic molecule. The role of the electrolytic solution used has been also discussed. Then, N-GQDs/ZnO hybrid has been elaborated and its photocatalytic properties toward the photoreduction of protons have been investigated and compared with those of the hybrid formed from non-doped GQDs.

Two graphite rods with a diameter of 6 mm (Ted Pella, Grade 1) were used as electrodes. After ultrasonic cleaning in water, they were immersed in 500 mL of milli-Q water and with a gap between them of 1 cm. The solution was then stirred and a voltage difference of 30 V was applied during 120 h. At the end of the electrolysis, the solution was centrifugated at 22,000 rpm during 20 min, in order to remove macroscopic fragments of graphite, and then filtered through different membranes (nylon 0,45 µm, nylon 0,2 µm and finally cellulose nitrate 0,1 µm). Finally, a black powder was obtained after freeze-dried lyophilization.
A similar protocol was employed for the synthesis of N-doped GQDs, by adding ammonia (Supelco, 25 % in water) in the solution in volume proportions 1:4.

ZnO was synthesized according to a method described in the literature [40]. Firstly, 1.49 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (Riedel, 5 mmol) was dissolved in 50 mL of milli-Q water. After complete dissolution, 1.6 g of NaOH (Acros, 40 mmol) was added in the solution under stirring. The solution was then maintained under stirring at 40 °C in a water bath during 6 h. The suspension obtained at the end of the reaction was centrifugated at 2500 rpm, washed three times with water, and then vacuum-dried though a nylon membrane with 0.2 µm pore diameter. The white powder was then dried in an oven at 40 °C and finally calcinated in a muffle oven at 500 °C during 1 h (and a temperature ramp of 10 °C.min−1). ZnO was finally obtained with a yield of 79 %.

ZnO was characterized by X-ray diffraction (Ka Co, Panalytical X'Pert Pro). ZnO obtained was crystallized as a pure hexagonal phase. The main peaks at 2q = 37.1°, 40,3° and 42.4° corresponded to (010), (011) and (002) planes, respectively.

2 mg of GQDs were added to 100 mg of ZnO in 300 mL of milli-Q water. The solution was sonicated for 5 min, and then stirred for 15 min. The solution was then transferred in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was heated at 140 °C in an electric oven for 4 h. The product was then recovered by vacuum-filtration through a nylon membrane with 0.2 µm pore diameters and washed thoroughly with water. The very slightly brown powder was finally dried in an oven at 45 °C overnight.

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations, several drops of an aqueous solution of the sample were deposited on an ultra-thin carbon coated 300 mesh copper grid (SPI Supplies), allowing it to dry between each deposition. Routine TEM measurements were performed on a JEOL 2100+ and high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) measurements were performed on a JEOL ARM 200 F operating both at 80 or 200 kV.

Raman samples were prepared by depositing a drop of solution on a silica substrate then left to dry overnight. Measurements were carried out by the Horiba LabRAM HR800 Raman. A helium-neon laser with wavelength at 633 nm was used as the excitation source. The spectrophotometer was coupled to a microscope with a 100X long distance objective (N.A.=0.9) for experiments in air. The power laser was between 0.5 mW (10 % filter) and 5.5 mW for (100 % filter) at the sample. All spectra were acquired with an acquisition time of 5 s and 3 accumulations.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed on a K Alpha+ spectrometer (ThermoFisher), equipped with a monochromated X-Ray Source (AlKα, 1486.6 eV). An X-ray spot size of 400 μm was used. The hemispherical analyzer was operated in CAE (Constant Analyzer Energy) mode, with a pass-energy of 100 eV and a step of 1 eV for the acquisition of survey spectra, while a pass-energy of 40 eV and a step of 0.1 eV were used for the acquisition of high resolution spectra. The spectra were recorded and data processing was performed with Advantage© software (V 5.99 from ThermoFisher). The elemental surface compositions were calculated by determining the area of each element of interest with a Shirley type background subtraction and using the Scofield sensitivity factors of the spectrometer. The spectra were calibrated against the C1s main peak component C-C/C-H set at 285.0 eV.

8 mg of ZnO/CDQs hybrids were put in suspension in 3 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.1 M of Na2S·9H2O and 0.1 M of Na2SO3 used as hole scavengers. Then, this suspension was placed in a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length, deaerated by bubbling argon and then sealed, and continuously stirred during experiment. Irradiation was performed using a 500 W-Xe lamp equipped with an optical fiber, an IR cut-off filter to avoid warming of the solution and, when required, an UV cut-off filter. At the end of the irradiation, the gaseous phase above the reaction medium in the quartz cuvette was analyzed using a gas chromatograph (Agilent Micro GC 990) equipped with a MS5A column.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of GQDs and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We have firstly investigated the synthesis of non-doped GQDs. A simple electrochemical setup was used, with two graphite rods employed as both the anode and the cathode. In order to avoid any other carbon source than the two graphite electrodes, milli-Q water was used as the electrolyte instead of any organic solvent, and without any other additive. A potential difference of 30 V was applied between the two electrodes. The current initially very low due to the absence of any electrolytic salt increased progressively while the solution became gradually orange until dark-brown after 48 h. After 5 days reaction under continuous stirring, the current reached 60 mA and the solution became completely black due to the detachment of large pieces of graphite from the electrode. Then, the electrolysis was stopped due to an almost total corrosion of the graphitic anode. After filtration of the solution to remove these large graphite particles in suspension, the resulting transparent yellow solution was finally lyophilizated to obtain a brown powder of pure non-doped GQDs (4 % in mass).

During the electrochemical reaction, gases were produced on both the anode and the cathode due to water splitting. Nevertheless, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) can also be generated from water on the anode surface at such high overpotential, causing the exfoliation of the graphite and the generation of GQDs [41–43].

The synthesis of the N-doped GQDs (named N-GQDs) was performed in adding ammonia (NH3) in ultrapure water during the electrolysis. Besides the fact that ammonia constitutes an excellent inorganic nitrogenous precursor, its use will also not require any particular separation step necessary when using organic molecules with high boiling points. In this context, NH3 appears as a good candidate as precursor to obtain N-GQDs. During this synthesis, a faster increase of the current was observed, probably due to the presence of NH4 + ions, to reach 300 mA after 2 days of electrolysis, just before stopping the synthesis due to a complete consumption of the electrode.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the non-doped GQDs exhibited in Fig. 1 A reveals uniform and monodisperse particles with a spherical morphology and a diameter around 4 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1B and C) show that the GQDs are highly crystalline, with lattice spacings of ca. 2.13 and 2.04 Å, assigned respectively to {100} and {011} planes of graphite (P63/mmc space group, ICDD no. 98-007-6767). In the case of N-GQDs (Fig. 1D, E and F), the particles appear more anisotropic with an increase of the size compared to non-doped GQDs (diameter ca. 10 nm). As previously described, the current measured during the electrochemical synthesis of the two kinds of GQDs was not the same, which may explain the difference in size and morphology of the nanoparticles obtained.

The Raman spectra of pristine graphite, non-doped GQDs and N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . For pristine graphite, three peaks are exhibited at 1350, 1580 and 2700 cm‒1, corresponding to the well-documented D, G and 2D peaks, respectively [44]. The G-band corresponds to the E2g modes of the graphite and is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The d-band corresponds to a breathing mode associated with defects in the graphitic structure leading to sp3-bonded carbon atoms and the vibrations of carbon atoms with disordered dangling bonds in the termination plane of graphite, while the 2D-band corresponds to the second order of the d-band. For GQDs and N-GQDs, the 2D peak disappears as generally observed in the literature while only the two prominent D and G bands are displayed. The intensity ratio I D/I G between D and G bands generally appears as a measurement of the disorder extent, as well as the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbons. In our case, the graphite has a weak I D/I G ratio around 0.3 while non-doped GQDs showed a noticeable increase in I D/I G ratio around 1.0. This increase in sp [3] carbons in GQDs is the result of the appearance of some structure defects such as the oxygenated groups on the sp [2] carbons during electrochemical oxidation of graphite. In the case of N-GQDs, the I D/I G ratio is even larger than that of non-doped GQDs (I D/I G = 1.2), which might be attributable to the presence of defects induced by the incorporation of nitrogen in the structure, as already observed in the case of nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes [45].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) provided a convincing proof for the N-doping of GQDs. Fig. 3 A and B depict the XPS survey spectra of non-doped and N-doped GQDs, respectively. We can observe a noticeable increase in the N(1s) signal around 400 eV for N-GQDs. In particular, the elemental nitrogen to carbon ratio N/C (measured using the C1s signal around 285 eV) increased from 0.05 for non-doped GQDs to 0.54 for N-GQDs. We can also notice that the O(1s) signal is much more intense in the case of N-GQDs. This could be explained by the higher current during the synthesis of N-GQDs, probably leading to more oxidized GQDs.

The N(1s) spectrum of N-GQDs is presented Fig. 4 A and can be deconvoluted into four peaks among which the peak centered at 399.4 eV should be attributed to pyridinic nitrogen (sp2 hybridized N bonding with two C atoms in a pyridine-like cycle, Fig. 4B) [46,47], the one at 400.3 eV should be assigned to pyrrolic nitrogen (sp3 hybridized N bonding with two C atoms in a pyrrole-like cycle, Fig. 4B) [46], and the one around 401.5 eV could correspond to graphitic nitrogen (quaternary N substituting C atoms in the hexagonal ring, Fig. 4B) [47]. Such attributions are in agreement with XPS measurements generally described in the literature for nitrogen-doped graphene or carbon nanotubes [48,49]. The last one centered at 406.6 eV corresponds to very oxidized nitrogen and could be assigned to nitro groups at the surface of GQDs, the anode which is the seat of the production of GQDs being at a very oxidizing potential.

The C(1s) XPS spectra of GQDs and N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . Concerning the non-doped GQDs, the deconvolution can lead to four peaks (Fig. 5A) [50,51]. The sharp peak at around 284.3 eV corresponds to the sp2 carbon atoms with C=C bonds as generally observed for graphitic materials, while the smaller peak at around 285.1 eV can be assigned to sp3 carbon atoms [49]. Three other peaks at higher energy can be attributed to oxidized carbon atoms, whose presence in high proportion (around 35 %) can be explained by the high potential applied to the electrode at which the GQDs are formed [53]. In particular, the peaks at 286.4, 287.6 and 288.7 eV in the deconvoluted C(1s) spectrum should be assigned to C‒O, C=O and O‒C=O bonds, respectively [48,52]. The generation of highly oxidized GQDs by this electrochemical method is confirmed by the presence of a large quantity of oxygen atoms observed in XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 3). Indeed, the GQDs are obtained at a very high electrochemical potential which can explain the presence of highly oxygenated groups.

For the N-GQDs, the deconvolution of the C(1s) signal shows also the presence of these five peaks, but in different proportions (Fig. 5B). Table 1 gives the proportions of the different kinds of bonds involving carbon atoms. We can observe an increase of oxidized carbons C=O and O‒C=O in the case of N-GQDs compared to GQDs. This is in agreement with the XPS survey spectra since we can clearly observe a higher quantity of oxygen in the case of N-GQDs. This can be explained by the fact that the current measured during this synthesis was higher than for the undoped GQDs. Moreover, it seems difficult to differentiate carbon atoms bonded to oxygen or nitrogen, even more when we have different possible hybridizations. The additional bonds involving nitrogen atoms are therefore hardly discernable but should contribute to the higher energy signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses in water vs. in ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs using an electrochemical process has been already described in the literature, but they are often performed in alkaline ethanol used as electrolyte [17,43,54]. These previous articles suggest that both alcohol and hydroxide anions played an important role in the process of GQDs formation.

In order to compare the role of the solvent, we therefore carried out the same synthesis of non-doped GQDs in ethanol using NaOH at a concentration of 20 g/L. After application of a potential difference of 30 V between the two graphite electrodes, the solution became instantly yellow and orange-brown after only a few minutes. After one hour, the solution was black and the graphitic anode was completely corroded. It therefore clearly appears that the reaction takes place much faster in alkaline alcoholic solution than in pure water. This can be easily explained by the presence of NaOH acting as an electrolyte salt. Nevertheless, after filtration and dialysis (to remove NaOH salt), it was impossible to obtain a dry powder of GQDs by evaporation of the ethanol; a slightly viscous residue was always observed. The formation of GQDs was confirmed by TEM (Fig. 6 A), however their observation was made difficult due to the presence of this viscous film at the surface of the grid.

This viscous compound was attributed to a by-product resulting from the alcohol during the synthesis. As a consequence, we performed an electrolysis at 30 V in the same alkaline alcoholic solution but in replacing graphite electrodes by two electrochemically inert platinum electrodes. Surprisingly, the color of the solution also changed very quickly, turning brown after a few minutes. After filtration and dialysis, the optical properties of the resulting solution were investigated. Interestingly, the solution emits in blue under UV illumination, like the classical GQDs. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra are red-shifted when the excitation wavelength increases (Fig. 6C). This tunable emission is described in the literature as a characteristic of GQDs, attributed to the heterogeneity of GQDs, such as broad size distribution and huge variety of surface chemical groups [55,56]. In comparison, the GQDs previously obtained in aqueous solution showed a similar characteristic optical behavior (Fig. 6D). XPS measurements were also performed on the powder obtained after lyophilisation: no platinum element was observed, eliminating the hypothesis of a possible contribution of this element in the fluorescence of the solution. Finally, the solution was analyzed by MET (Fig. 6B). TEM images show the presence of carbon nanoparticles. This is in agreement with the works of Deng et al. who have shown the formation of amorphous carbon nanoparticles directly from alcohol using an electrochemical method [57].

In consequence, electrochemical syntheses of GQDs classically described in the literature and performed in ethanol do not allow obtaining pure crystalline GQDs. Moreover, they should require complicated purification and separation procedures to obtain pure GQDs because ethanol leads to the formation of undesired by-products. These by-products have not been investigated in more details, but this is out of the scope of this study.

ZnO/GQDs hybrids have been obtained by grafting the GQDs (previously obtained in water) onto ZnO particles under solvothermal conditions at 140 °C. A band gap equal to 3.21 eV has been measured from Tauc plot (Fig. 7 ) for ZnO before addition of GQDs, which is consistent with the classical value generally reported in the literature [40,58]. After modification with GQDs, no significant change has been observed, the band gap increasing slightly up to 3.23 eV. We can note that no luminescence is observed, due to its quenching by ZnO.

Then, the photocatalytic properties of the hybrids towards water reduction have been investigated, using Na2SO3 Na2S sacrificial agent. A very small amount of H2 is produced with bare ZnO under UV–visible irradiation, around 1.3 µmol.g−1.h−1, which is in agreement with results generally obtained in the literature using such photocatalyst in these experimental conditions [40,59]. A significant increase in the quantity of H2 produced is observed when ZnO is decorated with GQDs. Indeed, when non-doped GQDs are used, a quantity of 19 µmol.g−1.h−1 of H2 has been obtained, while N-GQDs have remarkably improved photocatalytic hydrogen evolution by a factor of 66 compared to pristine ZnO, with 86 µmol.g−1.h−1 of H2 produced in this case. Under visible irradiation, as expected, the quantity of hydrogen produced has considerably decreased, due to the too high band gap value not allowing excitation in the visible domain. In particular in this case only 0.08 µmol.g−1.h−1 of H2 has been obtained with bare ZnO, while 0.84 µmol.g−1.h−1 has been produced with ZnO decorated with non-doped GQDs. Finally, an increase of a factor of 29 compared to pristine ZnO is observed with N-GQDs with 2,34 µmol.g−1.h−1.

Previous studies have reported a conduction band (CB) position of GQDs around -3.0 to -3.6 eV vs. vacuum energy level [19,60–62], which is higher in energy than CB position of ZnO (-4.2 eV vs. vacuum energy level [63]). Moreover, the valence band (VB) position of GQDs, generally reported around -5,3 to -6.0 eV vs. vacuum energy level [19,62], is higher in energy than the VB position of ZnO (-7.4 eV vs. vacuum energy level [63]). As a consequence, the band alignment of ZnO and GQDs results in a type-II heterojunction (Scheme 1 ), as classically described in the literature for such GQDs-based hybrids [19,64]. Under illumination, the photoexcitation of both ZnO and GQDs takes place, leading to electron-hole pairs formation. Then, transfer of photoexcited electrons occurs from the CB of GQDs to the CB of ZnO, preventing the electron-hole recombination generally observed in the case of pristine ZnO. Meanwhile, the photoinduced holes move from the VB of ZnO to the VB of GQDs, triggering efficiently the photoinduced electron-hole separation across the heterojunction. Finally, the electrons from the CB of ZnO react with protons to produce H2 while, on the other hand, holes in the VB of GQDs react with the sacrificial agent.

We have successfully synthesized GQDs using a top-down electrochemical approach using graphite as a carbonaceous precursor. While this synthesis is usually carried out in the literature in ethanol in the presence of sodium hydroxide, the GQDs are generally difficult to purify due to the formation of a slightly viscous by-product. In this context, we have chosen to carry out the same experiment in ultra-pure water and without electrolytic salt, the only source of carbon being the graphite electrodes used. Although longer, this synthesis effectively allows the production of pure GQDs in the absence of undesirable by-products. In addition, we have shown that it is possible to easily dope these GQDs with nitrogen by adding ammonia in the electrolytic solution. The prepared GQDs have been deposited onto ZnO and the resulting hybrids tested for the photocatalytic reduction of protons. The results are conclusive since a significant increase in the quantity of hydrogen produced is observed for the hybrids compared to pure ZnO. A mechanism based on a classical type-II heterojunction has been proposed to explain the reactivity. The production of hydrogen is further improved by the use of N-doped GQDs instead of non-doped materials, which shows a positive effect of this doping on the catalytic properties of GQDs. Other dopings may be considered in the future, as well as the preparation of hybrids with other semi-conductors with an absorption in the visible range.

Ari Gurel: Methodology, Investigation. Frédéric Avignon: Investigation. Guillaume Wang: Investigation. Stéphanie Lau: Investigation. Jean-Yves Piquemal: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Christian Perruchot: Formal analysis. Delphine Schaming: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Delphine Schaming reports financial support was provided by Paris Cité University. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Université Paris Cité and CNRS (Centre National de Recherche Scientifique). This project has received funding from the city of Paris (Emergence Project). Ari Gurel thanks DGA (Direction Générale de l'Armement) and ADEME (Agence de l'Environnement et de la Maîtrise de l'Energie) for his Ph.D fellowship. The authors would like to thank Philippe Decorse for XPS measurements. XPS equipment was funded by the Région Ile-de-France, convention SESAME n°16016303 and the Labex SEAM (Science and Engineering for Advanced Materials and devices).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xia, C.; Zhu, S.; Feng, T.; Yang, M.; Yang, B. Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots. Adv. Sci. 2019, 6 (23), 1901316. 10.1002/advs.201901316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
